topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The package arrived just as the rain began to lash against the windowpanes. I found it tucked beside my worn copy of poetry: a bouquet of deep purple irises, their velvet petals impossibly dark against the crisp white paper. There was no note. Just the flowers. My hands, usually steady, started to tremble as I unfolded the wrapping. A cold prickle crawled up my arms. Who would do this? I stared at the arrangement, the vibrant blooms seeming too loud, too insistent in the quiet gloom of my apartment. Every shadow in the room seemed to deepen, and a strange tightness constricted my chest, making each breath shallow and catching in my throat. I backed away from the door, my heart hammering a frantic rhythm against my ribs, convinced whatever this was, it wasn't meant for me.

[story 2]

It was placed on my doorstep at precisely midnight. A cascade of bright yellow lilies sat there, untouched by any accompanying message. I hesitated, my shadow stretching long and distorted across the hallway floor. The air outside felt strangely heavy, charged with an unspoken tension. When I finally reached for the flowers, my fingers brushed against the cool stems, and a sudden wave of alarm washed over me. My gaze darted around the dimly lit entryway, searching for an observer, a sign that this was a trick. A tremor ran through my legs, making me unsteady on my feet. I snatched the bouquet, clutching it to my chest as if it might ward off some unseen presence. The silence of the building amplified the sound of my own ragged breathing.

[story 3]

The box was unassuming, brown cardboard, and inside, a collection of roses—crimson and startlingly bright. No inscription. As I lifted the box, a shiver traced its way down my spine, a visceral reaction that made my skin crawl. I stood in the middle of my living room, the flowers seeming to pulse under the weak lamp light. My jaw tightened, and I couldn't seem to focus on anything else; my eyes kept flicking towards the closed front door, expecting someone to materialize. A knot formed in my stomach, a heavy, sickening weight. I slowly lowered the box, my movements stiff, and whispered a question into the empty room, my voice barely audible, "Who sent these?"
